summary: In this advice column, Aunt Pythia addresses four reader questions covering sexism at academic conferences, dating app culture, career anxiety in data science, and workplace dynamics for female faculty. She provides practical, direct advice on being an active bystander against harassment, defends her critique of treating dating like an optimization problem, reassures a statistician that Kaggle competitions miss the real skills employers need, and coaches a female professor on navigating subtle sexism from support staff. The column blends humor with substantive guidance on navigating gendered professional challenges.
key_points: Active bystanders should escalate intervention progressively: talk to the harasser, check in with the target, and involve organizers if needed
Kaggle competitions are poor proxies for real data science because they pre-define problems and success metrics—the actual job is figuring out what questions to ask
Workplace sexism often manifests in small, deniable interactions that accumulate over time
Loving one or two people per day through speed-dating is emotionally impossible, distinct from casual sex which she supports
Female academics should create 'small but useful confrontation' rather than passively accepting gendered treatment
money_quotes: As soon as someone responds with a 'not interested' signal, it is of course the responsibility of the interested party to tone it down.
Make him try to think about her perspective rather than his own dick's perspective.
Hiring people who are good at Kaggle competitions is like hiring the chef who has been supplied with a menu, a bunch of recipes, and all the ingredients to run your new restaurant.
It's not actually all that impressive to be able to follow directions, you need to be creative and thoughtful.
I don't think loving one or two people per day is possible.
I don't know why you are 'rigorously avoiding confrontation' here.
Do not let this stuff build up inside you! Make it an amusing part of your day to see how people wiggle out of their responsibilities.
key_insight: Being a good ally against workplace sexism requires escalating action rather than passive sympathy, while real professional competence lies in asking the right questions rather than optimizing pre-defined metrics.
